expectations.text: F1: Flags routes.ts importing drizzle-orm/db schema and querying the DB directly inside the route handler (presentation touching infrastructure)
expectations.evidence: Finding #1: 'Route handler queries Drizzle directly ... This is presentation-layer code importing infrastructure directly — the exact anti-pattern in examples.md #1', cites dependency-cruiser rule 'handlers-never-touch-the-db'

expectations.text: F2: Flags the endpoint-URL uniqueness check as business logic misplaced in the route handler
expectations.evidence: Finding #1: 'the duplicate-URL check here (existing.some(...)) is a business rule (uniqueness invariant), and it's sitting in the transport layer instead of the domain/application layer'

expectations.text: F3: Flags claimDueRetries' backoff/MAX_ATTEMPTS logic as a domain rule embedded in the repository/infrastructure layer
expectations.evidence: Finding #6: 'Business rule (retry eligibility / backoff / dead-lettering) implemented inside the repository ... Retry-eligibility and backoff computation is exactly this kind of workflow decision — it's a domain invariant... not a data-access concern'

expectations.text: F4: Flags repository methods returning raw Drizzle rows outward with no row->entity/DTO mapping
expectations.evidence: Finding #7: 'Repository returns raw Drizzle rows outward instead of mapping to domain entities/DTOs' — also independently catches that the leaked row round-trips the `secret` column back to the client

expectations.text: F5: Flags service.ts constructing a concrete OpenAI client directly instead of going through the LLMProvider port/container
expectations.evidence: Finding #4: 'Application service owns a concrete OpenAI client instead of depending on an LLMProvider port ... This is the clearest violation in the module', cites SKILL.md tool->layer mapping verbatim

expectations.text: F1: Flags routes.ts importing drizzle-orm/db schema and querying the DB directly inside the route handler (presentation touching infrastructure)
expectations.evidence: Finding #1 and #2: 'Routes hand-roll a DB query and bypass the repository/service entirely' + 'Route reads t (db schema) and eq from drizzle-orm — infrastructure leaking into presentation', compared against sibling agents/routes.ts

expectations.text: F2: Flags the endpoint-URL uniqueness check as business logic misplaced in the route handler
expectations.evidence: Finding #1: 'Business logic ("an endpoint URL must be unique per workspace" — the 409 check) is implemented in the routes file, not the service, so it can't be reused or unit tested independently of Fastify'

expectations.text: F3: Flags claimDueRetries' backoff/MAX_ATTEMPTS logic as a domain rule embedded in the repository/infrastructure layer
expectations.evidence: Finding #5: 'Retry-window/backoff policy (business rule) implemented in the repository ... This is domain/business logic ... sitting inside the repository, which per the onion/clean split should be pure data access'

expectations.text: F4: Flags repository methods returning raw Drizzle rows outward with no row->entity/DTO mapping
expectations.evidence: No finding addresses row->DTO mapping or the repository leaking Drizzle's raw row shape outward; the summary table has no entry for it, and the secret-column round-trip to the client is never mentioned anywhere in the review

expectations.text: F5: Flags service.ts constructing a concrete OpenAI client directly instead of going through the LLMProvider port/container
expectations.evidence: Finding #4: 'Direct OpenAI client construction and module-level singleton in the service — should go through the LLM adapter/container', contrasts with agents/service.ts's container.llm(provider) pattern

notes: Single run per configuration (n=1) — no variance data; treat the pass-rate delta as directional, not statistically robust.

The only assertion that discriminated between conditions was F4 (raw Drizzle rows leaking outward with no DTO mapping) — without-skill missed it entirely, including the secondary security implication that the `secret` column round-trips back to the client. F1/F2/F3/F5 were caught by both conditions, largely because the without-skill agent proactively compared against the sibling `agents/` module for house conventions (a crutch that won't exist for a brand-new module with no sibling to imitate).

With-skill found 3 legitimate issues beyond the seeded 5 (DI/composition-root violation, hand-rolled Zod casts instead of schema-first params, and the complete absence of a domain layer/repository interface) — all traceable to specific reference docs (references/dependency-injection.md, references/domain-layer.md) rather than inferred from house style, giving more precise 'where should this go' guidance.

Without-skill found 2 legitimate issues beyond the seeded 5 (fetch() has no error handling for thrown network errors; the retry route's unused/unvalidated :endpointId param) — both real but tactical/correctness nits rather than architecture-layer findings.
With-skill took +23.6s and +13k more tokens for the extra rigor — worth it here since this is a pre-merge architecture gate, not a latency-sensitive path.

Caveat: the without-skill agent was still fairly strong because it was explicitly told to skim server/CLAUDE.md and a sibling module. A true 'zero context' baseline (no CLAUDE.md, no sibling module) would likely show a larger gap — this benchmark specifically isolates the skill's incremental value on top of already-good repo hygiene, not skill-vs-nothing.
